instances.name: Bethlehem Steel executive culture
instances.summary: Bethlehem Steel operated an elite executive dining room with premium food service, elaborate bureaucratic control systems for employee communications, and strict office decorum rules that discouraged innovation.
instances.key_quote: at bethlehem steel they had we had a manual that thick that told me who i could write a letter to within the company okay

instances.name: Bethlehem Steel I-beam rolling and Manhattan skyline development
instances.summary: Bethlehem Steel's innovation in rolling I-beams around 1900 enabled tall building construction; before this, brick-and-mortar buildings were limited to 10-12 stories.
instances.key_quote: some people say that the growth of Manhattan was because of Bethlehem Steel learning how to make ey beams until around 1900 buildings never went above 10 or 12 stories

instances.name: Bethlehem Steel tool steel alloy innovation
instances.summary: Bethlehem Steel learned to alloy steel with molybdenum around 1900, creating tool steels that could retain strength at high temperatures for applications like drill bits.
instances.key_quote: it had learned to alloy steel around 1900 with malum and they learned to make tool Steels that could take keep their strength at very high temperatures like drill bits

instances.name: Bethlehem Steel mill construction
instances.summary: Bethlehem Steel built the world's last large integrated steel mill between 1965-1970 with ingot casting technology instead of continuous casting, requiring a five to six billion dollar investment that nearly bankrupted the company.
instances.key_quote: when bethlehem steel built the world's last steel mill built by a company in the 1960s from 65 through 70 took him about five years to build it probably about a five or six billion dollar investment just about bankrupt the largest company the second largest steel company

instances.name: Bethlehem Steel ingot caster investment
instances.summary: Bethlehem Steel invested $5-6 billion in ingot casting technology in the 1960s, nearly bankrupting the second-largest steel company in the world, only to realize within a decade that continuous casting was the superior technology.
instances.key_quote: in the 1960s from 65 through 70 took him about five years to build it probably about a five or six billion dollar investment just about bankrupt the largest company the second largest steel company in the world bethlehem steel
instances.framework_lesson: Steel industry managers were unwilling to take technological risks when economically viable; they repeated old practices for 80 years despite emerging superior alternatives.

instances.name: Bethlehem Steel suicide incident
instances.summary: A retired worker at Bethlehem Steel returned to the facility and jumped into a molten steel bath, with his body floating on the surface and later an ingot buried in the cemetery due to phosphorus contamination from human remains.
instances.key_quote: when i was at bethlehem steel one guy retired and then he decided to commit suicide so he came back and jumped into the bath you float very well on steel

instances.name: Bethlehem Steel near-bankruptcy on integrated mill construction
instances.summary: Bethlehem Steel spent its entire year's gross product (approximately $5 billion in 1965) building a new integrated steel mill and nearly went bankrupt, though the facility later became their most profitable asset.
instances.key_quote: it cost bethlehem steel in 1965 5 billion dollars that was their whole year's gross product okay and they almost went bankrupt but by the time they hired me it was the most profitable year ever

instances.name: Bethlehem Steel mill construction (1965)
instances.summary: Bethlehem Steel built an integrated steel mill in 1965 at Burns Harbor for approximately 5 billion dollars, nearly bankrupting the company but eventually becoming highly profitable.
instances.key_quote: it cost bethlehem steel in 1965 5 billion dollars that was their whole year's gross product okay and they almost went bankrupt

instances.name: Bethlehem Steel I-beam rolling and New York City skyscrapers
instances.summary: Bethlehem Steel's development of I-beam rolling technology around 1900 enabled the transition from 10-story brick and wooden buildings to steel-frame skyscrapers in New York City.
instances.key_quote: the reason new york city went from 10 story buildings to skyscrapers was because bethlehem steel learned how to roll i-beams around 1900

instances.name: Bethlehem Steel yield improvement case
instances.summary: Bethlehem Steel's executive Mike Kimchuk spent his career trying to improve casting yield from 63% to higher levels, with each 1% improvement potentially increasing company profits by 30%.
instances.key_quote: if he could get a 1% yield betham Steels profits were probably increased by 30% okay just to get a 1% increase in what we call yield

instances.name: Bethlehem Steel Depression-era executive compensation scandal
instances.summary: During the 1930s Depression, Bethlehem Steel chairman Eugene Grace and executives were paid bonuses based on tons of steel poured, not sold, incentivizing overproduction while the company lost money.
instances.key_quote: Eugene Grace and a number of his cronies were getting paid a bonus on the number of tons of steel poured not the number of tons of steel sold

instances.name: Bethlehem Steel Quincy Shipyard LNG tanker certification fraud
instances.summary: Bethlehem Steel supplied steel for LNG tankers to meet a 20 foot-pound Coast Guard specification; when shipyard workers found occasional plates exceeding 30 foot-pounds, they cut those superior plates into test coupons to falsely demonstrate compliance, rather than using representative material from the actual hull plates.
instances.key_quote: when they found this one plate that would come in once a month with 30 foot pounds of toughness what do they went and cut it up into run off tabs so what they were measuring was not the toughness of what was going to go in the ship they were measuring the toughness of the best plate they could find
instances.framework_lesson: Poorly designed specifications with marginal safety margins incentivize fraud; test coupons cut from off-spec material can bypass quality verification intended to ensure hull integrity.

instances.name: Bethlehem Steel hospital for maimed workers
instances.summary: Bethlehem Steel built a hospital specifically to serve workers who had lost limbs or suffered serious injuries in steel mill accidents, illustrating the dangerous working conditions in early-to-mid 20th century integrated steel mills.
instances.key_quote: wonderful hospital where my second child was born because the steel company had built the hospital for all the iron workers who had lost their arm or their leg you know in accidents

instances.name: Bethlehem Steel bankruptcy
instances.summary: Bethlehem Steel, which operated the Burns Harbor Indiana steel mill, went bankrupt after Eagar began consulting with them.

instances.name: Bethlehem Steel Burns Harbor plant
instances.summary: Bethlehem Steel (world's second-largest steel company) built the Burns Harbor Indiana plant in 1965 at a cost of approximately $5 billion in 1965 dollars (equivalent to $20 billion in the lecture year), marking the last greenfield steel mill built by a private company in the United States.
instances.key_quote: the last steel mill built Green Field site built in this country was built by betham Steel world's second largest steel company uh it was Burns Harbor Indiana plant now I think it's owned by AR Nal but that was in 1965

instances.name: Generator rotor forging (US Steel or Bethlehem Steel)
instances.summary: A massive 450-ton generator rotor forging, reduced through forging to ~200 tons, was produced at a major US steel plant (possibly Bethlehem Steel) using procedures originally developed for World War I battleship gun barrels.
instances.key_quote: this was basically a system where they made Battleship gun Barrels in World War One okay um not a lot of facilities like that in the world a lot of crane capacity a lot of big height

instances.name: Bethlehem Steel plant closure and conversion

instances.key_quote: Bethlehem is now in a that steel plant is now in a amusement park in Bethlehem Pennsylvania

instances.name: Generator rotor forging welding project at Bethlehem Steel
instances.summary: Eagar was asked in the 1970s to develop technology for welding two large forgings together to create 700-ton generator rotors, as only two U.S. facilities and a few international sites could heat-treat and machine single forgings of that size.
instances.key_quote: i was asked when i went to work for bethlehem steel in the 1970s to figure out how to take two foragings and weld them together because the heat treatment technology to make that generator order forging there were only two places in the united states that could do it

instances.name: Charpy impact testing at Bethlehem Steel
instances.summary: As a 25-year-old engineer at Bethlehem Steel, Eagar produced Charpy bar samples showing brittle versus ductile fracture modes, with some tough steels repeatedly requiring recalibration of the 260 foot-pound impact hammer.
instances.key_quote: these sharpie bars that i actually produced when i was a engineer a 25 year old engineer at bethlehem steel and there's half of one which is a brittle fracture and the other one is the ductile fracture

instances.name: Bethlehem Steel management collapse (1973–1975)
instances.summary: Bethlehem Steel, which controlled 75% of world steel capacity in 1945, by 1973 had only 25% market share; managers hired in 1945 still believed they controlled 75% and continued monopoly-era pricing and decision-making into a competitive market.
instances.key_quote: the American steel industry owned 75% of the world steel making capacity in 1945 cuz we had bombed out most of the rest it's great you just bomb out your competition there's a good businessman
instances.framework_lesson: Organizational culture and mental models formed under artificial market dominance persist long after market conditions change, causing strategic paralysis in face of real competition.

instances.name: Bethlehem Steel management decline
instances.summary: Bethlehem Steel's senior management in 1975 believed they still controlled 75% of world steel capacity (based on 1945 dominance) when they actually controlled only 25%, leading to strategic delusion and company decline.
instances.key_quote: all the managers who had been hired as young employees in 1945 were now running the company and they still had the attitude that they controlled 75% of the world steel business

instances.name: Bethlehem Steel financial accounting fallacy
instances.summary: A Bethlehem Steel financial vice president claimed old furnaces and coke ovens made the company more efficient because equipment was fully depreciated, revealing fundamental misunderstanding of true operational efficiency versus accounting treatment.
instances.key_quote: we are one of the most efficient steel companies in the world cuz our blast furnaces were built in 1912 and our Coke Ovens were built in 1911... because it's fully depreciated

instances.name: Bethlehem Steel Alaska pipeline project
instances.summary: At Bethlehem Steel in the 1970s-1980s, engineers designed crack arresters (inserts of thicker, high-toughness steel like A710) to be installed every couple hundred yards in large-diameter pipelines to prevent 30-mile running fractures.
instances.key_quote: when i worked for bethlehem steel they were starting to build the alaskan pipeline and there were other pipeline projects people were building in the world
instances.framework_lesson: The cost and supply constraints of high-toughness alloys (A710 with high nickel content) made it impractical to build entire pipelines from premium materials; instead, engineers adopted crack-arrester technology at intervals.

instances.name: Bethlehem Steel yield and profitability analysis
instances.summary: Bethlehem Steel, the second-largest steel company in the world in 1974, operated at a 65% overall product yield across all divisions, and modeling showed that improving yield to 66-67% could double corporate profitability.
instances.key_quote: when I started at betham Steel in 1974 the overall product yield was about 65% for the whole Corporation all products and betham was second largest steel company in the world at the time

instances.name: Bethlehem Steel yield improvement and industry overcapacity
instances.summary: Bethlehem Steel and the entire U.S. steel industry experienced a crisis of overcapacity and price competition in the 1970s–1980s when switching from ingot casting to continuous casting unexpectedly increased productivity and manufacturing capacity by approximately 50% industry-wide.
instances.key_quote: all of a sudden if in a 10-year period you were producing one-third more steel than you needed in the casting shop because now you switch to continuous casting
instances.framework_lesson: Disruptive improvements in manufacturing yield can create catastrophic overcapacity and competitive pressure that nearly bankrupts an entire industry despite technological progress.

instances.name: Bethlehem Steel I-beam innovation and New York City skyline
instances.summary: Bethlehem Steel's development of I-beam rolling technology enabled tall building construction in New York City, transforming Manhattan's skyline from flat to vertical.
instances.key_quote: the reason New York City now has buildings more than six stories tall is because betham steel made I beams

instances.name: Bethlehem Steel I-beam rolling and NYC skyscraper development

instances.name: Bethlehem Steel hot-work tool steel development
instances.summary: Bethlehem Steel innovated hot-work tool steels using molybdenum and molybdenum carbides in the 1890s-1910s, enabling growth of the automotive industry.
instances.key_quote: they came up with hot work tool steels using molybdenum and molybdenum carbides that allowed a lot of the growth of the automotive industry

instances.name: Burns Harbor Steel Mill
instances.summary: Bethlehem Steel Corporation built a $5 billion integrated steel mill at Burns Harbor, Indiana starting in 1964, which was one of the most modern steel plants in the world but nearly bankrupted the company.
instances.key_quote: an integrated steel mill is what we call today the last one ever to be built by a private company was burns harbor in indiana started in 1964 by Bethlem steel corporation it cost them about 5 billion dollars they were the second largest steel company in the world and it almost bankrupt did them
instances.framework_lesson: Large capital investment in conventional blast furnace technology did not account for impending process innovation; the company became overextended before basic oxygen furnace technology displaced open hearth methods.

instances.name: Bethlehem Steel ingot vs. continuous casting decision
instances.summary: Bethlehem Steel installed ingot casting at Burns Harbor despite continuous casting being available, due to risk aversion; they had to rip out and replace the facility within 10 years.
instances.key_quote: Bethlem steel put in ingot casting at burns harbor even though continuous casting was sort of coming along because they didn't want to take the risk armco steel did the same thing okay they didn't want to take the risk within 10 years of putting in the billion dollar facility
instances.framework_lesson: Risk-averse capital investment decisions by incumbents led to stranded assets when superior technology proved economically dominant through yield improvement.

instances.name: Bethlehem Steel labor and productivity transition (1975-1980s)
instances.summary: Bethlehem Steel experienced a dramatic tenfold increase in steel productivity (from ~10 person-hours per ton in 1975 to <0.6 in the 1980s), which drove employment in U.S. steel industry from 500,000 workers in 1980 to 250,000 by 1990 while maintaining 100 million tons annual production.
instances.key_quote: when i was at Bethlem steel it was just under ten person hours per ton okay he's like six as i remember in 1975 and now it's at less than point six a tenfold increase in like 30 or 40 years

instances.name: Bethlehem Steel Burns Harbor Plant

instances.name: Bethlehem Steel Accounting Misconception
instances.summary: A Bethlehem Steel finance vice president told new hires that fully depreciated 1911-1912 equipment made steel at zero cost, ignoring actual productivity losses.
instances.key_quote: the vice president finance got up and he told us that it caught cost bethlehem nothing to make steel because we had 1911 coke ovens and 1912 blast furnaces
instances.framework_lesson: Depreciation accounting does not reflect actual manufacturing efficiency; old equipment does not improve productivity despite being fully depreciated.

instances.name: Bethlehem Steel Burns Harbor mill
instances.summary: A major new steel mill built by Bethlehem Steel at Burns Harbor in the 1960s nearly bankrupted the company, but became highly productive and profitable by 1974, representing the best year in Bethlehem Steel's history.
instances.key_quote: the last company built steel mill in the world that i know of was bethlehem steel burns harbor almost bankrupt bethlehem steel in the 1960s but by 1974 once burns harbor started high productivity turned out steel is the best year bethlehem steel ever had

instances.name: Bethlehem Steel shipyard weld-over-primer project
instances.summary: Bethlehem Steel commissioned research to develop a weld metal capable of welding over paint primer in shipyard practice, as paint contains phosphorus that causes solidification cracking.
instances.year_or_era: early 1970s (Eagar's first project at Bethlehem Steel)
instances.key_quote: one of my first projects was to well tom how can we come up with a weld metal that can weld over paint well people have been trying to do this for 50 years knowing to solve the problem and i didn't solve it either
instances.framework_lesson: Paint-based corrosion protection (phosphorus-rich coatings) is incompatible with welding metallurgy; specification changes to primer composition are more practical than developing special weld metals.

instances.name: Bethlehem Steel high-toughness testing

instances.name: Bethlehem Steel Burns Harbor plant

instances.name: Bethlehem Steel plant management mindset (1975)
instances.summary: Bethlehem Steel leadership in 1975 believed their fully depreciated 1910-era coke ovens and 1911 blast furnaces cost nothing to operate, leading to competitive failure against modern Japanese mills.

instances.framework_lesson: Accounting depreciation schedules mask true operational costs; managers conflated zero book value with zero production cost, blinding them to competitive disadvantage.

instances.name: Bethlehem Steel forging plant slag removal (1975)

instances.name: Bethlehem Steel multi-facility management study

instances.key_quote: you had different plant managers different philosophies and that attitude from the top permeated down through some of them were Theory X type of managers some of them were Theory Y type of managers and you could very quickly tell

instances.name: Bethlehem Steel quality improvement proposal (1975)

instances.framework_lesson: Management metrics focused on production volume rather than profitability or quality created perverse incentives that ultimately contributed to the company's decline.

instances.name: Bethlehem Steel cyanide emissions

instances.name: Bethlehem Steel financial misunderstanding
instances.summary: A Bethlehem Steel vice president of finance explained that the Lackawanna plant with coke ovens built in 1910 and blast furnaces from 1911 had no operating costs because they were fully depreciated, revealing fundamental misunderstanding of operating economics versus accounting depreciation.

instances.framework_lesson: Integrated steel producers confused accounting depreciation with actual operating costs, failing to recognize that 80-year-old facilities were far less efficient than postwar Japanese equipment.

instances.name: Bethlehem Steel—process improvement rejection

instances.framework_lesson: Corporate culture that prioritizes volume over quality leads to competitive disadvantage and long-term business failure.

instances.name: Bethlehem Steel offshore oil platform laminar tearing
instances.summary: Heavy steel plates 3-4 inches thick used in offshore oil platforms built in the mid-1970s experienced laminar tearing problems due to high sulfur content and large oxide inclusions in the steel.
instances.key_quote: When I worked at Bethlehem Steel in the mid70s, the oil companies were building uh a lot of big offshore oil platforms. They were big heavy steel plates 3 and 4 in thick. They were getting laminitarian problems because the steel wasn't very clean.
instances.framework_lesson: Steel-making technology of the 1960s and before produced high-sulfur steel (400 ppm) that caused laminar tearing; modern facilities and Japanese methods demonstrated inexpensive production of cleaner steel (50 ppm sulfur) eliminating the problem.

instances.name: Bethlehem Steel offshore oil platform lamellar tearing
instances.summary: Heavy steel plates (3–4 inches thick) for offshore oil platforms built in the mid-1970s experienced lamellar tearing due to high sulfur content and large oxide inclusions in the steel.
instances.key_quote: When I worked at Bethlehem Steel in the mid70s, the oil companies were building uh a lot of big offshore oil platforms. They were getting laminitarian problems because the steel wasn't very clean.
instances.framework_lesson: Modern Japanese steelmaking with clean furnaces (50 ppm sulfur) made lamellar tearing obsolete, whereas outdated blast furnace technology (1911-era) at U.S. mills produced 400 ppm sulfur steel prone to lamellar cracking.

instances.name: Bethlehem Steel reheat cracking in stress relief
instances.summary: Steels cracked during stress-relief heat treatment (1220°F for 1 hour) due to trace impurities (sulfur, arsenic, phosphorus, antimony) forming brittle intergranular fractures.
instances.key_quote: it turns out sometimes they would come out and they actually hear a pow inside the furnace and it was a great big crack going through your steel and you'd have this very inner granular crack
instances.framework_lesson: Reheat cracking was a persistent failure mode through the 1970s until cleaner steelmaking eliminated the impurity problem; the issue required both materials science understanding and manufacturing process control.

instances.name: Bethlehem Steel financial mismanagement
instances.summary: Bethlehem Steel's finance vice-president misunderstood financial principles regarding equipment depreciation, contributing to the company's eventual bankruptcy despite operating one of the world's largest integrated mills.
instances.key_quote: he said well that's cause you don't understand finance while I later learned that this vice-president finance didn't understand finance and that's why Bethlem steel is bankrupt now

instances.name: Bethlehem Steel high-tensile steel testing

instances.framework_lesson: Reference material standards and machine recalibration protocols can become operationally impractical when materials exceed assumed performance envelopes.

cluster_id: Northridge earthquake
display_name: Northridge earthquake

instances.name: Northridge earthquake structural damage
instances.summary: The 1994 Northridge earthquake in California caused approximately 5 billion dollars in damage to static structures because building codes had no criteria for energy absorption under vibration.
instances.key_quote: the Northridge earthquake in the early 90s in California all the codes and standards had designed to force a fracture and they had no real criteria for energy a fracture and you come along and have these static structures that get vibrated in an earthquake and it caused five billion dollars with a damaged
instances.framework_lesson: Building codes specified strength but not toughness or energy-absorption capacity; codes were subsequently rewritten to require vibration-energy absorption.

instances.name: Northridge earthquake building collapse
instances.summary: A Southern California building failed catastrophically during the 1994 Northridge earthquake, causing billions of dollars in damage, due to steel structures lacking fracture toughness specifications in the building code.
instances.key_quote: when the Northridge earthquake came to Southern California in the 1990s the building snapped in the earthquake and caused billions of dollars worth of damage
instances.framework_lesson: Building codes based on strength alone, without energy-of-fracture (impact) criteria, failed to prevent brittle failure; codes were not updated for 40 years after Irwin's fracture mechanics work in the 1950s.

instances.name: Northridge earthquake building failures
instances.summary: The 1994 Northridge earthquake in Southern California caused significant structural failures in welded steel buildings, leading to a major revision of steel design codes and the development of load-and-resistance-factor design (LRFD) methodology.
instances.key_quote: they learned a lot of this by looking at the failures of buildings in the northridge earthquake a lot of this came out of northridge
instances.framework_lesson: Major earthquake-induced structural failures drove the adoption of new probabilistic design frameworks (LRFD) that separately quantify load-side and material-side safety factors rather than combined global factors.

instances.name: Northridge earthquake building failures
instances.summary: Building failures during the 1994 Northridge earthquake led to major revisions in steel construction design codes and safety factor approaches.
instances.key_quote: they learned a lot of this by looking at the failures of buildings in the northridge earthquake a lot of this came out of northridge
instances.framework_lesson: Building failures in Northridge prompted the American Institute of Steel Construction to adopt load and resistance factor design (LRFD) methodology that separates safety factors on load and material sides.

instances.name: 1994 Northridge earthquake building failures
instances.summary: A magnitude 6.7 earthquake in Southern California caused 5-10 billion dollars in building damage because structural steel lacked fracture toughness specifications, despite the Navy requiring such specs for ships since the 1950s.
instances.key_quote: we didn't have specifications for high toughness in buildings until the nineteen ninety northridge earthquake in southern california that did five to ten billion dollars with the damage because the steels didn't have a fracture toughness specification
instances.framework_lesson: Civil engineering codes lagged 40 years behind naval standards because no regulatory body forced toughness requirements into building specifications until after catastrophic earthquake damage.

instances.name: Northridge earthquake and moment connection failures
instances.summary: The 1994 Northridge earthquake in Los Angeles exposed failures in moment connections (T-intersections of floor beams and columns) in building frames, causing building and bridge collapses when subjected to lateral seismic motion.
instances.key_quote: after the 1990s the civil engineers were doing a huge amount of redesign of the code for um seismic applications that's one of the reasons the codes get thicker and thicker with time
instances.framework_lesson: Static design codes (supporting vertical weight) do not account for lateral dynamic loads from earthquakes; seismic failures drove major code revisions for moment connections and building frame behavior under oscillation.
instances.source_module: Codes and Standards Definitions and History

instances.name: Northridge earthquake steel connection failures
instances.summary: The 1994 Northridge earthquake in Southern California caused 20 billion dollars of damage, partly because civil engineers had not incorporated seismic fracture toughness requirements into building codes; steel connections snapped during vibration.
instances.key_quote: we had an earthquake in southern california called the northridge earthquake 20 billion dollars worth of damage they had never put into their codes and standards a seismic real seismic requirements
instances.framework_lesson: Civil engineers did not learn and apply fracture mechanics principles until 1994; building codes lacked seismic toughness requirements for steel connections, leading to catastrophic failures.

instances.name: Northridge earthquake
instances.summary: The 1994 Northridge earthquake revealed brittle fracture problems in steel structural connections, forcing civil engineers to confront fracture mechanics principles.
instances.key_quote: the civil engineers had it with the northridge earthquake
instances.framework_lesson: Structural design codes had not adequately incorporated fracture toughness criteria for seismic loading and welded steel connections.

instances.name: Northridge earthquake building code revisions
instances.summary: The 1994 Northridge earthquake in Southern California exposed building design deficiencies and prompted code updates.
instances.key_quote: The North Ridge earthquake in Southern California um when there's a major problem they often revise the code or standard to make things safer
instances.framework_lesson: Major natural disasters reveal gaps in existing codes and standards, leading to revisions that address previously unencountered failure modes.
instances.source_module: Codes and Standards Definitions and History

instances.name: Northridge earthquake steel building damage
instances.summary: The 1994 Northridge earthquake in Los Angeles exposed weaknesses in steel building design that had not been updated since the 1920s, prompting engineers to develop modern allowable stress design standards.
instances.key_quote: after the northridge earthquake in night early 90s like 91 or 92 in los angeles the steel construction folks went back and said let's get the design out of the 1920s and bring it into the 1980s
instances.framework_lesson: Outdated design philosophy that was too weak in some areas and too strong in others was replaced by modern allowable stress design standards following earthquake failures.

instances.name: Northridge earthquake building failures
instances.summary: Buildings constructed in the 1960s with inadequate fracture energy design experienced connection failures during the 1994 Northridge earthquake, causing billions in damage.
instances.key_quote: civil engineers got it in the 1990s because of the Northridge earthquake and they found that buildings built in the 60s when we knew about the need for fracture energy but it wasn't part of the code
instances.framework_lesson: Engineering knowledge about fracture energy existed decades before it was mandated in building codes; codes lag behind material science until catastrophic failures occur.

instances.name: Northridge earthquake structural response
instances.summary: The early 1980s Northridge earthquake in Southern California revealed that buildings designed as static structures experienced unexpected dynamic loading and frequency coupling with soil liquefaction; fewer than 100 deaths occurred because modern building codes had already incorporated earthquake design principles.
instances.key_quote: in the north ridge earthquake they all of a sudden became dynamically loaded structures as the building swaying in the breeze...the foundation the soil can turn to jello
instances.framework_lesson: Static design safety factors are insufficient for seismic loading; toughness and dynamic response factors, informed by earlier earthquakes (San Francisco 1906), prevented widespread collapse.

instances.name: Oakland Bay Bridge collapse (Northridge earthquake)

instances.name: Northridge earthquake

instances.framework_lesson: Building codes based on traditional beam formulas for a century did not account for brittle fracture under dynamic impact; Northridge forced adoption of Load Factor Resistance Design (LFRD) methodology.

instances.framework_lesson: Structural design codes had focused on strength criteria without adequately accounting for fracture energy absorption capacity under dynamic loading.

instances.name: Northridge earthquake building failures
instances.summary: The 1994 Northridge earthquake in California caused approximately $20 billion in structural damage, highlighting the importance of toughness in structural design.

instances.framework_lesson: Engineers had not adequately considered fracture toughness as a design criterion when selecting structural materials.

instances.name: Northridge earthquake building and bridge damage

instances.name: Chaparral Steel net-shape casting innovation
instances.summary: Chaparral Steel developed continuous casting molds that produced near-net-shape I-beam structural sections, reducing manufacturing costs by one-third compared to traditional ingot casting methods.

instances.name: Chaparral Steel mini-mill innovation and product unification
instances.summary: Chaparral Steel's new wide flange mill in the mid-1990s used net-shape casting and in-line reheat to reduce processing time from days to 28 minutes, enabling them to produce A36 composition steel that achieved A50 strength specifications while reducing U.S. steel imports to zero and exporting 20 percent of production.
instances.key_quote: if you were smart enough you could order 36 ksi and get 50 ksi for free because they got to the point where it was just as easy to make 50 ksi material as it was to make 36 ksi material
instances.framework_lesson: Fine-grain microstructure achieved through faster, lower-temperature rolling provided both strength and toughness, making traditional grade distinctions obsolete without requiring dual inventory systems.

instances.name: Chaparral Steel

instances.name: Chaparral Steel failures

instances.name: Chaparral Steel rebar splitting innovation

instances.name: Chaparral Steel rebar and structural shapes innovation
instances.summary: Gordon Ford founded Chaparral Steel in Texas around 1975, recognizing arbitrage between scrap steel ($100/ton) and cast iron production cost ($180/ton), and began developing novel slab designs with notches to reduce rolling and eventually produce structural shapes beyond simple reinforcing bar.
instances.key_quote: gordon ford who's graduated this department back in 1975. he saw the hundred dollar scrap price versus 180 cast iron price and he was one of the guys said you can make some money there

instances.name: Chaparral Steel four-strand rebar rolling

instances.name: Chaparral Steel I-beam near-net-shape casting innovation
instances.summary: Chaparral Steel developed near-net-shape casting and optimized rolling processes to reduce I-beam production from 21 passes to 7 passes, achieving superior properties, eliminating steel imports, and capturing 75% market share in the early 1990s.

cluster_id: Seawolf submarine hydrogen cracking
display_name: Seawolf submarine hydrogen cracking

instances.name: Seawolf-class HY100 cylinder installation and cracking
instances.summary: Two Seawolf-class submarines were equipped with HY100 steel cylinders before the main class experienced widespread cracking, prompting inspection protocols for the installed modules.
instances.key_quote: my first student ever still works at electric boat as a welding engineer he was in charge of welding up those hy1 100 cylinders and when the seaw wolf had all its cracking problems the two captains who were in charge of the ships came to me
instances.framework_lesson: Transition to higher-strength steels without fully resolved hydrogen cracking mitigation created risk in operational vessels requiring post-delivery inspection protocols.

instances.name: Seawolf-class HY130 feasibility challenge (Navy planning)
instances.summary: The Navy attempted to design the Seawolf-class as an HY130 ship despite unresolved hydrogen cracking problems, which Eagar advised against in a 1990 meeting with the Chief Engineer of the Navy.
instances.key_quote: I remember having to go meet with the chief engineer of the na Navy in around 19 90 and explaining to them that you couldn't they were trying to sell the Seawolf as it was going to be an H y130 ship and I said no it's going to be a big enough challenge to go to Hy 100
instances.framework_lesson: Naval procurement attempted to use materials (HY130) that lacked demonstrated welding and cracking control solutions, requiring expert intervention to redirect toward technically feasible specifications.

instances.name: Sea Wolf submarine weld cracking
instances.summary: Hydrogen cracking occurred in weld metal rather than heat-affected zone due to overmatching weld metal strength, surprising engineers who expected cracks only in the HAZ.
instances.key_quote: in the sea wolf where it occurred in the weld metal which was a big surprise because it had always occurred in heat affected zone before
instances.framework_lesson: Design and welding standards had not anticipated hydrogen cracking in weld metal when strength-matched or overmatched consumables were used on high-strength steels.

instances.name: Seawolf submarine hydrogen cracking
instances.summary: USS Seawolf submarine experienced hydrogen cracking failures during welding of HY-100 steel due to weld metal chemistry being at maximum ranges, creating martensite instead of austenitic ferrite, costing approximately $2 billion in repairs.
instances.key_quote: the carbon was at the maximum of the range the manganese was the maximum of the range the chromium everything was at the maximum of the range and they were getting 130 KSI weld metal it was more of a martensitic rather than austenitic ferrite and all of a sudden they ran into all kinds of problems with hydrogen cracking cost about 2 billion
instances.framework_lesson: Welding specifications that allow chemistry to range to maximum values across all alloying elements can unexpectedly produce hard martensite phases prone to hydrogen cracking rather than the intended austenitic ferrite structure.

instances.name: Seawolf submarine weld cracking
instances.summary: A Navy Seawolf submarine had undetected brittle microcracks in weld metal due to unintended high carbon and alloy content; 18% of the hull was built before discovery.
instances.key_quote: they the weld metal was 30 or 40% higher strength than it was intended to be because of the chemistry of the weld metal and they got a less tough steel
instances.framework_lesson: Inspection standards were calibrated for large flaws (1/8 inch) but missed millimeter-scale brittle cracks; a non-standard grinding inspection technique revealed the defect.

instances.name: Seawolf consulting engagement
instances.summary: Tom Eagar was brought in as one of six consulting experts (two individuals, four companies) to investigate hydrogen cracking problems in Seawolf HY-100 welds; he concluded the root cause was drawing lubricant on the welding wire surface.
instances.key_quote: they brought us in early on there were six of us there were two of us who were individuals and there were four companies and uh one of the individuals who had been head of welding at one of the big Electro manufacturers got bounced early on because he spoke the press
instances.framework_lesson: Welding wire procurement specifications must control not only chemical composition and mechanical properties but also surface cleanliness, as drawing lubricant residue on finer-diameter wires introduces hydrogen embrittlement risk in high-strength submarine steels.

instances.name: Sea Wolf submarine HY-100 vs HY-80 steel sections integrity
instances.summary: The Navy had built some sections of earlier Sea Wolf submarines with newly qualified HY-100 high-strength steel instead of the established HY-80; captains needed assurance that small sections built from HY-100 would not require retirement from service.
instances.key_quote: they wanted to know if they were going to have to deer ate these submarines that are out there floating around in the ocean and I said no no oh you don't have to worry about it fracture mechanics says that the critical flaw size is probably three or four inches in your steel
instances.framework_lesson: Fracture mechanics analysis can justify continued operation of components built from new, higher-strength materials by demonstrating that critical flaw sizes exceed actual defect populations, avoiding costly decommissioning.

instances.name: Sea Wolf submarine welding wire problem
instances.summary: Navy submarine welding wire from a segregated casting ingot had high-side chemistry with excessive alloying elements, increasing yield strength to 140 KSI and hydrogen sensitivity, causing a $2 billion problem.
instances.key_quote: the whole Sea Wolf problem $2 billion it cost the Navy was because the welding wire was too rich it had too much of certain alloe elements
instances.framework_lesson: Navy specifications allowed a wide range for alloy composition to account for casting inhomogeneities, but segregation from dendritic structures meant some lots fell at the high end with drastically different hydrogen sensitivity.

instances.name: Seawolf submarine microcracks
instances.summary: During construction of the first Seawolf-class submarine, a grinding operator discovered a family of microcracks in the welds smaller than 1/16 inch, leading to discovery that the entire vessel was contaminated with similar defects traced to high-chemistry weld wire.
instances.key_quote: the whole ship was full of these things and they tracked it down to the weld wire was on the high side chemistry
instances.framework_lesson: Inspection codes set minimum flaw detection thresholds (1/8 inch) based on realistic detection capabilities, but novel weld defect modes fell below this threshold, revealing that codes lag behind emerging failure mechanisms.

instances.name: Seawolf submarine consulting engagement
instances.summary: Tom Eagar provided consulting services to the Chief Engineer of the Navy on the Seawolf submarine project, where he negotiated consulting rates directly with government rather than through intermediaries.
instances.key_quote: i've worked on projects where i like the seawolf submarine working for the chief engineer of the navy no one asked me about my consultant they just said tell us what the rate is

instances.name: Seawolf submarine welding hydrogen cracking
instances.summary: Hydrogen cracking occurred during welding of the USS Seawolf nuclear submarine, which Eagar traced to lubricant contamination as the hydrogen source.
instances.key_quote: I can tell you a story about welding of the Seawolf submarine and I tracked it down in my opinion to lubricants

instances.name: SeaWolf submarine hydrogen cracking
instances.summary: The USS Seawolf submarine hull experienced extensive hydrogen cracking in the weld metal discovered during construction around 1991-1992, requiring rework of 18 percent of the hull due to microcracks undetectable by standard radiography.
instances.key_quote: they've got rip it all out we're finding these little micro cracks in the weld metal it wasn't in the heat affected zone 18 of the holes built
instances.framework_lesson: Navy inspection protocols qualified for detecting eighth-inch cracks could not find sixteenth-inch weld metal cracks; detection standards were calibrated to larger defect sizes based on probability-of-detection studies rather than actual failure-critical dimensions.

instances.name: Seawolf-class submarine HY-100 qualification testing
instances.summary: Navy tested HY-100 steel sections installed in prior 688-class submarines as qualification testing before full Seawolf construction, then consulted Eagar on whether existing ships needed to be de-rated due to small cracks found in welds.
instances.key_quote: they said uh we got a couple of subs out there that have hy100 in them in the hall what should we do we have to derate them
instances.framework_lesson: Fracture mechanics principles show that small 1/16-inch cracks grow too slowly between inspection intervals to pose operational risk, allowing continued safe operation without de-rating.

instances.name: SeaWolf submarine hydrogen cracking problem
instances.summary: The SeaWolf submarine required extreme preheat temperatures (400°F) and specialized cooling suits for welders to prevent hydrogen cracking due to high-strength steel and poor hydrogen control.
instances.key_quote: when they had the problem with the seawolf submarine these guys had to go into enclosed containers where the steel had to be at 400 degrees fahrenheit to prevent the hydrogen cracking they had to wear blue jelly suits
instances.framework_lesson: High-strength steel specifications without adequate hydrogen control created impractical welding conditions that required extraordinary (and expensive) engineering workarounds rather than material or design changes.

instances.name: Seawolf submarine hydrogen cracking (MIG welding)
instances.summary: The Seawolf submarine experienced hydrogen cracking problems during construction, specifically related to gas metal arc (MIG) welding processes used on high-strength steel.
instances.key_quote: the Seawolf submarine the problem was with MIG welding okay gas metal arc welding where you had no flux
instances.framework_lesson: High-restraint submarine structures require extended post-weld waiting periods (up to one week) before non-destructive testing because hydrogen cracks can form days after welding is complete.

instances.name: Sea Wolf submarine excessive weld overmatching
instances.summary: USS Sea Wolf experienced higher residual stresses and hydrogen cracking risk due to excessive overmatching of weld metal (30–40% higher strength than base metal) combined with insufficient preheat for the resulting high hardenability.
instances.key_quote: they ended up having a very high material strength in the weld metal too high it was actually over matching by about 30 or 40 percent
instances.framework_lesson: Overmatching weld metal by more than ~20% increases residual stress and hardenability to levels that require proportionally higher preheat; failure to adjust preheat leads to hydrogen cracking.

instances.name: Seawolf weld cracking from high-side chemistry
instances.summary: The first 18% of a Seawolf-class submarine vessel experienced weld cracking because the welding wire heat was made with high-side alloy composition (effectively HY-130) instead of specified HY-100.
instances.key_quote: every this heat of steel that they made the welding wire out of for the first 18 percent of the vessel was on we called a high side chemistry and it was really more like an hy130 and they should have been doing hy130 welding procedures because the weld metal you know was basically hy130 and that's why they got cracking
instances.framework_lesson: Material specifications with loose compositional ranges (e.g., carbon 0.08–0.16) can result in weld metal properties that drift dangerously from intended hardenability and crack resistance; welding procedures must match actual alloy chemistry.

cluster_id: Soviet Alpha-class submarine
display_name: Soviet Alpha-class submarine

instances.name: Alpha-class submarine welding investigation
instances.summary: Eagar conducted research on how Soviet submarines were welded, particularly titanium construction, which led to investigations into flux-assisted TIG welding techniques used in their fabrication.
instances.key_quote: I hadn't applied it to titanium until after the alpha sub stuff when we're trying to figure out how they built that

instances.name: Soviet Alpha-class submarine
instances.summary: The Soviet Union deployed the alpha-class all-titanium attack submarines in 1980, ahead of US capability, which could dive deeper and move faster than US vessels, but the submarines were mothballed within two years due to fatigue cracking and excessive noise from crack propagation.
instances.key_quote: titanium subs could dive deeper than the collapse depth of our depth charges okay they could go faster underwater than our destroyers could go on the surface
instances.framework_lesson: Titanium's creep-fatigue interaction at room temperature in ocean environments was not adequately addressed in the design, causing premature fatigue failures.

instances.name: Soviet Alpha-class submarine fatigue cracking
instances.summary: Soviet Alpha-class submarines developed fatigue cracks in titanium hulls during operation, making them acoustically detectable and eventually forcing their retirement.
instances.key_quote: they tended to develop fatigue cracks okay and they would make a lot of noise because these cracks would start rubbing inside
instances.framework_lesson: Naval engineers failed to account for creep-fatigue interaction in titanium at ocean temperatures, a phenomenon that required room-temperature testing protocols not initially applied.

instances.name: Soviet Alpha-class titanium submarine
instances.summary: The Soviet Union built a titanium submarine (Alpha-class) announced publicly in 1980; it achieved superior depth and speed capabilities but developed creep-fatigue interaction cracks because the Soviets did not anticipate or solve the problem of crack growth under sustained compression fatigue that occurs in submarine hulls at depth.
instances.key_quote: they didn't they just built the ships and then they found out about the creep fatigue interaction which nrl had studied and knew about
instances.framework_lesson: The Soviet Navy built the titanium submarines without understanding or designing for creep-fatigue interaction under compression; the U.S. Naval Research Laboratory had already studied this failure mode but it was a major barrier preventing U.S. titanium submarine development.

instances.name: Alpha-class submarine decommissioning
instances.summary: Soviet-era alpha-class submarines were taken out of service due to cracking, excessive noise, and high maintenance costs.
instances.key_quote: they were being laid up okay mothballs well yeah okay yeah basically mothballed being taken out of duty okay uh and being mothballed uh because they had cracks and they were noisy as could be and they're expensive to maintain

instances.name: Alpha-class submarine titanium hulls
instances.summary: The Soviet Union deployed the first all-titanium attack submarines in 1980, prompting the U.S. Navy to reconsider titanium for submarine hulls after 30 years of research.
instances.key_quote: when the alpha sub was first kind of came on the scene 1980 right the alpha sub everybody know what the alpha sub is the office sub was the attack submarine all titanium attack submarines the Soviets dropped on the world in 1980 and it was a real eye-opener for the U.S Navy

instances.name: Alpha submarine titanium welding challenge
instances.summary: After the Soviet Union announced the Alpha-class attack submarine in 1980, which could dive deeper than U.S. depth charges and travel faster than surface destroyers, the U.S. Navy faced a strategic crisis and launched intensive research into heavy-section titanium welding technology.
instances.key_quote: in 1980 I was flying back from a conference in Europe and I read on the front page of the international Herald Tribune that the Soviets have the alpha submarine this is their new attack submarine it could dive deeper than the collapse depth of our deepest depth charge
instances.framework_lesson: The Navy and civilian aerospace industry held completely divergent definitions of what constituted 'heavy-section' titanium (1 inch vs. 3/8 inch), revealing a critical gap between military specifications and industrial capability.

instances.name: Soviet Alpha-class submarine titanium welding
instances.summary: Soviet Alpha-class submarines built with titanium hulls developed stress corrosion cracking within three years that made them acoustically detectable, negating their stealth advantage and requiring them to be taken out of service.
instances.key_quote: they cracked Soviets never studied it apparently they didn't know about it they built several submarines and within three years they were in Dry Dock
instances.framework_lesson: The Soviets did not identify or study the creep-fatigue interaction vulnerability in titanium that the U.S. Naval Research Lab had recognized as an Achilles heel, leading to premature failure of their fleet.

instances.name: Soviet Alpha-class submarine
instances.summary: The Soviet Union built a titanium attack submarine (Alpha-class) that could dive deeper and move faster than U.S. vessels, but developed extensive cracking problems and became too brittle to operate safely within a few years.
instances.key_quote: within 3 to four years you didn't have any problem tracking it because it didn't go out of the harbor because it had so many cracks that you couldn't dive it
instances.framework_lesson: The Soviet Naval Research Laboratory identified fracture problems with titanium that they had not solved, yet proceeded with construction anyway, leading to premature structural failure.

instances.name: Soviet Alpha-class submarines
instances.summary: Soviet Alpha submarines built with titanium hulls developed extensive cracking that prevented them from diving, despite initial superior speed and depth capabilities compared to U.S. destroyers.
instances.key_quote: within 3 to four years you didn't have any problem tracking it because it didn't go out of the harbor because it had so many cracks that you couldn't dive it
instances.framework_lesson: Soviet naval research identified but failed to solve titanium fracture problems; the Navy chose to build the ships anyway rather than resolving the engineering issue, resulting in vessels with severely limited operational lifetime.

instances.name: Alpha submarine and Navy composite submarine program
instances.summary: The Navy attempted to develop an all-composite submarine after the Alpha submarine, but Congress initially rejected the titanium submarine design and redirected funds to DARPA for alternative materials research in the mid-1980s.
instances.key_quote: the Navy wanted to build an all composite submarine after the alpha sub and many people in the Navy says well they went up to the hill and told Congress we'll build a titanium submarine and Congress said no we're not going to be second

instances.name: Soviet Alpha-class submarine cracking
instances.summary: Soviet Alpha-class submarines cracked in service due to creep-fatigue interaction in their titanium hulls, despite initially outpacing U.S. submarine technology.
instances.key_quote: the alpha sub came out the soviets built the alpha became public knowledge that they had the alpha sub in about 1980 and they leapfrogged us in submarine technology for about two years until these things started cracking due to the creep fatigue interaction

instances.name: Soviet Alpha-class submarine titanium welding
instances.summary: Soviet submarine designer Durevich developed methods to weld thick-section titanium (one to four inches) for the Alpha-class submarine using electric slag welding, derived from Soviet tank repair techniques.
instances.key_quote: i all of a sudden realized how durevich had welded a lot of the alpha sub because i was thinking back to having read his papers prior to 1972 and he had talked a lot he'd written a lot of papers on electric slag welding
materials_or_systems: one to four inch thick sections

instances.name: Soviet Alpha-class submarine titanium welding
instances.summary: The Soviet Union built titanium submarines using electro-slag welding and gas tungsten arc welding processes while the US Navy spent millions trying unsuccessfully to use gas metal arc welding for titanium.
instances.key_quote: the Soviets used electro slag and another process they used was gas tungsten arc welding and the US Navy had been working on a process that the Soviets had determined a long time ago didn't work
instances.framework_lesson: The US Navy pursued a welding process (GMAW) for titanium submarines that Soviet metallurgists had already determined was ineffective, wasting resources on an unviable technical path.

instances.name: Alpha submarine titanium welding

instances.name: Soviet Alpha-class submarine

cluster_id: Space Shuttle Challenger
display_name: Space Shuttle Challenger

instances.name: Space Shuttle Challenger foam insulation strike
instances.summary: A piece of foam insulation from the external tank struck and damaged the Space Shuttle, leading to catastrophic failure and loss of the vehicle.
instances.key_quote: the space shovel should not have fallen that was not nice one silly little piece of foam should not bring down that giant machine
instances.framework_lesson: Even seemingly minor components can cause total system failure; physics does not care about assumptions of redundancy or safety margins.
instances.source_module: How to be a Successful Engineer

instances.name: Space Shuttle Challenger O-ring failure
instances.summary: The Space Shuttle Challenger exploded during launch due to O-ring failure caused by cold temperatures; engineers at Alliant Technology warned against launch in 28-degree weather but were overruled due to political pressure from a planned presidential address by a teacher-astronaut.
instances.key_quote: the engineer says it's too cold the o-rings are too stiff because they're so cold it's not going to work and they had previous history of the o-ring damage
instances.framework_lesson: Engineering safety recommendations were overridden by political and schedule pressures; O-ring design lacked adequate temperature margin despite prior damage history at 40 degrees.

instances.name: Space Shuttle Challenger O-ring failure
instances.summary: The Space Shuttle Challenger launched in cold weather conditions (28 degrees), with engineers knowing it had never flown that cold before, leading to O-ring failure and disaster.
instances.key_quote: they said oh well we've done we've flown cold missions before yeah but never this cold okay but that's just someone trying to gloss it over
instances.framework_lesson: Engineering teams glossed over the fact that previous cold-weather missions had never approached the critical temperature threshold, allowing a known material limitation to be minimized rather than treated as a hard constraint.

instances.name: Space Shuttle Challenger O-ring failure
instances.summary: Space Shuttle Challenger experienced a catastrophic failure related to O-ring leaks, which influenced materials selection for the Space Shuttle Main Tank.
instances.key_quote: they found out with space shuttle Challenger what happened with leaks of the o-ring

instances.name: Space Shuttle Challenger O-ring failure
instances.summary: The Space Shuttle failed in 1986 not at a weld but at a joint—the O-ring seal—demonstrating that joint failures are the primary failure mode in complex systems.
instances.key_quote: when the space shuttle blew up in 1986 or whatever my first comment was i bet it failed at a joint actually i said i better failed at a weld i was wrong it didn't fail at a well but it did fail the joint it was no ring seal

instances.name: Space Shuttle Challenger disaster
instances.summary: A major space vehicle failure mentioned as an example of catastrophic engineering failure that forces industry rethinking.
instances.key_quote: like the space shuttle challenge or someone brought that up the other day

instances.name: Space Shuttle Challenger O-ring failure

instances.key_quote: Space Shuttle failing on launch okay and then when it failed on launch Fineman was part of the blue ribbon panel that was supposed to figure out what's going on and they learned it was the o-rings you know in the cold weather

instances.name: Space Shuttle Challenger disaster and reliability calculations
instances.summary: Space Shuttle Challenger failed on its 25th flight; NASA had claimed 1-in-10,000 failure rate but actual historical failure rate was 4%, exposed by Richard Feynman on the investigation commission.
instances.key_quote: NASA supposedly because of these calculations claimed the space shuttle was going to be 0.01 not 4% reliable but point oh one percent reliable and Feynman pointed out it just happened to be the 25th shuttle flight so that's your 4%
instances.framework_lesson: NASA's reliability calculations grossly underestimated failure rates compared to actual historical data from all previous rocket flights; statistical models cannot override empirical failure evidence.

instances.name: Space Shuttle Challenger disaster
instances.summary: The Space Shuttle Challenger failed on launch in 1986 due to O-ring seal failure in the solid rocket motor that allowed hot gases to blow past the seals.

instances.name: Space Shuttle O-ring failure (Challenger)
instances.summary: Space Shuttle Challenger launched in freezing temperatures on January 28, 1986, resulting in loss of vehicle and crew due to O-ring failure in solid rocket motor joint that Morton Thiokol engineers had warned against.
instances.key_quote: the engineers at Morton Thiokol they had been successful in 24 flights and they went to the thiokol managers and said well we don't think you should fly tomorrow
instances.framework_lesson: Engineering recommendations were overridden by management and political pressure; lack of accountability in presentation design (unnamed preparers) and failure to adequately communicate risk data contributed to the disaster.
instances.source_module: How to be a Successful Engineer

instances.name: Space Shuttle Challenger disaster
instances.summary: Space Shuttle Challenger (SRM-25) was launched on January 28, 1986, in cold weather conditions (29°F), causing O-ring failure and catastrophic loss of vehicle and crew.
instances.key_quote: they finally decided to cut off the conference call and just have the Thiokol managers talk to the engineers and one vice president fire Cole told Bosco Lee go take off your engineer hat and put on your managers hat
instances.framework_lesson: Engineers had data showing O-ring damage correlated with temperature, but poor data visualization and managerial pressure to launch obscured the failure risk that proper graphical analysis would have revealed.
instances.source_module: How to be a Successful Engineer

instances.name: Space Shuttle Challenger
instances.summary: Space shuttle failed in 1986, but not at a weld as Eagar initially predicted; it failed at an O-ring joint.
instances.key_quote: when I heard about the space shuttle Challenger what in 1986 or whatever my students told me about it that morning and I said oh I bet it failed at a weld and I was wrong but it did fail at an O-ring joint
instances.framework_lesson: Critical failures often occur at joints, whether welded or otherwise, because joints are typically the highest stress locations in structures.

instances.name: Challenger disaster and Space Shuttle cost overruns
instances.summary: The Space Shuttle program failed to achieve its goal of reducing launch costs to $1,000 per pound in orbit; the Challenger disaster forced a shutdown that drove fixed costs up dramatically as flights per year went to nearly zero.
instances.key_quote: the space shuttle was supposed to reduce the cost to a thousand dollars a pound in orbit anybody have an idea how close they got to that yeah well yeah with the challenger disaster they shut down everything

instances.name: Space Shuttle Challenger disaster
instances.summary: The Challenger disaster resulted in shutdown of Space Shuttle operations, which when divided across zero flights per year dramatically increased the per-pound cost to orbit.
instances.key_quote: well yeah with the challenger disaster they shut down everything and when you divide by zero flights a year your fixed costs are still there and you get a very big number

instances.name: Space Shuttle Challenger
instances.summary: The Space Shuttle Challenger disaster, referenced as a parallel risk case where a structural leak in the spacecraft design led to catastrophic failure.
instances.key_quote: if you ever got a hole a leak it's you know the space shuttle Challenger all over again kaboom cause you got hydrogen and air at 4,000 degrees it burns
instances.framework_lesson: Designs with high-consequence failure modes (catastrophic risk from small defects) require orders-of-magnitude more stringent quality and tolerance control than low-consequence structures.

instances.name: USS Thresher disaster
